perturbation.original_text: The Joint Venturers shall maintain adequate books and records to be kept of all the Joint Venture activities and affairs conducted pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Joint Venturers should endeavor to maintain records of the Joint Venture activities and affairs conducted pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall maintain adequate books and records' to 'should endeavor to maintain records' weakens the obligation to keep accurate and complete records, making it discretionary. The removal of 'adequate' also introduces ambiguity regarding the level of detail required.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding business records and fiduciary duties
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law requires businesses to maintain proper records and books. By changing the requirement from 'shall maintain' to 'should endeavor to maintain', the modified text suggests that maintaining records is optional, contradicting the mandatory nature of the law and potentially violating the fiduciary duties of the joint venturers.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the Joint Venturers 'shall maintain adequate books and records,' which creates a clear legal obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'should endeavor to maintain records', introducing ambiguity and suggesting that maintaining records is optional. This is a legal contradiction because it undermines the requirement to keep accurate and complete records, potentially violating the fiduciary duties of the joint venturers, as explained in the LLM Law Explanation. The absence of supporting snippets doesn't negate the fact that weakening such language introduces ambiguity, and contradicts legal norms requiring responsible record keeping in business operations.

perturbation.original_text: All direct costs and expenses, which shall include any insurance costs in connection with the distribution of the Products or operations of the Joint Venture, or if the business of the Joint Venture requires additional office facilities than those now presently maintained by each Joint Venturer, such item shall be paid by the Joint Venture.
perturbation.changed_text: Direct costs and expenses, including insurance costs potentially related to product distribution or Joint Venture operations, and possibly additional office facilities if needed, may be considered for payment by the Joint Venture.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that certain costs *shall* be paid by the Joint Venture. The modified text changes 'shall include' to 'potentially related' and 'if the business...such item shall be paid...' to 'possibly additional office facilities if needed, may be considered for payment', making the payment of these costs discretionary. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania contract law requires that agreements are interpreted according to their plain meaning. The original clause imposes a clear obligation for the Joint Venture to pay certain costs. The modified text introduces ambiguity and discretion ('may be considered'), which could be interpreted as a violation of the original agreement's intent and creates a less binding commitment, making it potentially unenforceable under Pennsylvania law if these costs are incurred.
perturbation.justification: The original contract language created a clear obligation for the Joint Venture to pay certain costs ('shall be paid'). The changed text replaces this with discretionary language ('may be considered'), weakening the original obligation. This introduces ambiguity, potentially contradicting the original agreement's intent, as described in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation confirms that the payment of costs, previously mandatory, is now discretionary.

perturbation.original_text: If requested by a Joint Venturer, the Joint Venture books and records shall be audited as of the close of each year by an independent accountant acceptable to both Joint Venturers.
perturbation.changed_text: If requested by a Joint Venturer, the Joint Venture books and records might be audited as of the close of each year by an accountant.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates an audit by an 'independent accountant acceptable to both Joint Venturers' if requested. The revised text replaces 'shall be audited' with 'might be audited' creating uncertainty about whether the audit will occur at all, and removes the requirement that the accountant be 'independent' and 'acceptable to both Joint Venturers'. This introduces significant ambiguity about the scope and quality of any potential audit.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law regarding fiduciary duties in joint ventures
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law imposes fiduciary duties on joint venturers, which include acting in good faith and with due care. An independent audit, conducted by an accountant agreed upon by both parties, ensures transparency and accountability, satisfying these duties. By making the audit discretionary and removing the independence requirement, the modified text weakens these safeguards and could be construed as a breach of fiduciary duty by failing to ensure proper oversight of the Joint Venture's finances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Section 8313 - Partnership property :: 2015 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes :: US Codes and Statutes :: US Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes 2024 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2023 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2022 Penn. Cons. Stat. 2015 Penn. Cons. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2015 Pennsylvania Consolidated Statutes Title 15 - CORPORATIONS AND UNINCORPORATED ASSOCIATIONS Chapter 83 - General Partnerships Section 8313 - Partnership property 15 PA Cons Stat § 8313 (2015) What's This? § 8313. Partnership property. (a) General rule.-- All property originally brought into the partnership stock or subsequently acquired, by purchase or otherwise, on account of the partnership is partnership property. (b) Acquisition with partnership funds.-- Unless the contrary intention appears, property acquired with partnership funds is partnership property. (c) Property in partnership name.-- Any estate in real property may be acquired in the partnership name. Title so acquired can be conveyed only in the partnership name. (d) Extent of interest acquired.-- A conveyance to a partnership in the partnership name, though without words of inheritance, passes the entire estate of the grantor unless a contrary intent appears. perturbation.justification: The original text mandates an audit ('shall be audited') by an independent accountant if requested. The changed text introduces ambiguity by using 'might be audited,' making the audit discretionary and removing the requirement for an independent accountant. Although the provided law snippet discusses partnership property, the law explanation suggests that the modified text weakens fiduciary duties by potentially failing to ensure proper financial oversight. This aligns with the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition, as the ambiguity created makes it difficult to enforce transparency and accountability, potentially breaching fiduciary duties.
